title: Federal Politician (MP)
setting: Parliament House, Canberra + Electorate Office, Sydney
description: A sitting federal MP splits their time between Canberra (parliament sitting weeks — roughly 20 weeks/year) and their electorate office. In Canberra, the day revolves around caucus, committees, Question Time, and votes. In the electorate, it's constituent meetings, community events, and local media. The role demands constant context-switching between national policy and local concerns.

schedule.activity: Morning media scan — read overnight coverage, check social media mentions, prepare for any emerging issues
schedule.note: The news cycle never sleeps. Being across the morning coverage before your first meeting is non-negotiable.

schedule.activity: Breakfast meeting with a stakeholder group — industry lobby, union delegation, or community organisation
schedule.note: Breakfast meetings are prime real estate in Canberra. Everyone wants 30 minutes with an MP.

schedule.activity: Caucus or party room meeting — discuss party strategy, upcoming bills, and internal politics
schedule.note: What happens in caucus stays in caucus. This is where real party decisions are made.

schedule.activity: Committee hearing — questioning public servants or witnesses on a specific policy area
schedule.note: Committees are where the detailed policy work happens, away from the theatrics of Question Time.

schedule.activity: Lunch meeting with a colleague to build support for a private member's motion or bill amendment
schedule.note: Much of parliament's real work happens in informal conversations over lunch and coffee.

schedule.activity: Question Time — sit in the chamber, ask or answer questions, respond to points of order
schedule.note: Question Time is theatre, but it sets the news cycle. A strong 30-second grab can dominate the evening news.

schedule.activity: Debate and votes in the chamber — speak on legislation, participate in divisions
schedule.note: You must be present for divisions (votes). The party whip tracks attendance.

schedule.activity: Media interview or doorstop — respond to the day's developments for evening news bulletins
Reception or fundraising event — network with colleagues, donors, or stakeholders
schedule.note: Evenings in Canberra are filled with events. Saying no is as important as saying yes.

schedule.activity: Preparation for tomorrow — read briefing papers, review committee documents, respond to emails
Call family — a Canberra week means being away from home Monday to Thursday
schedule.note: The personal cost of federal politics is significant. Most MPs are away from their families half the year.

responsibilities: Represent your electorate's interests in the federal parliament
Scrutinise government legislation and spending through committees and debate
Manage an electorate office with 4-5 staff handling constituent casework
Attend parliament sitting weeks (approximately 20 per year)
Participate in party caucus and policy development
Engage with local media, community groups, and constituents
Fundraise for your re-election and your party
challenges: Constant travel between Canberra and your electorate disrupts family life
Balancing national party discipline with local electorate needs
24/7 media scrutiny — any mistake becomes a news story
Managing constituent expectations when you cannot fix everything
Internal party politics can be more challenging than opposing parties
rewards: Direct ability to influence national legislation and policy
Helping individual constituents navigate government services
Being part of debates that shape the country's direction
Platform to champion causes you believe in
The privilege of representing a community that chose you
careerPath: Local community organiser or party volunteer
Party branch member and preselection candidate
Senior Minister, Deputy Leader, or Prime Minister
